content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

August 26, 1993

Phil Kohn
City Attorney
City of Laguna Beach
P.O. Box 1950
Costa Mesa, CA 92628-1950

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-93-294

Dear Mr. Kohn:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on
behalf of Laguna Beach Councilmember Ann Christoph regarding the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").¹ Since you request is one for general guidance, we are
treating it as a request for informal assistance.²

Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

Also, the Commission does not have jurisdiction over other
laws which may be applicable to your facts, such as laws governing
incompatible activities or Section 1090. You should consult the
Attorney General's Office for further guidance.

QUESTION

May Councilmember Christoph participate in city council
decisions relating to the disposition of a city park and a

_______________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000 et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs.
Section 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-93-294
Page 2


proposed reservoir, where she has received more than $250 from the
city within the last 12 months to perform architectural landscape
work at the park?

CONCLUSION

The City of Laguna Beach is a source of income to
Councilmember Christoph.  Therefore, she may not participate in
city council decisions which will have a reasonably foreseeable
and material financial effect on the city.  However, if the effect
of the decisions flow to all, or a significant segment, of the
residents of the City of Laguna Beach, Councilmember Christoph
will not be required to disqualify herself from participating in
the city council decisions.

FACTS

Councilmember Christoph was elected in 1990 to the Laguna
Beach City Council.  She also is the sole proprietor of a
landscape architectural firm.  In 1988, she, one other landscape
architect, and an engineer were jointly awarded a contract by the
city for the performance of various professional services in
connection with a city park.  The architects and engineer were
treated as independent contractors by the city, and each one was
paid directly by the city for performance of his or her component
of the work under the contract.

The contract work was performed between 1988 and 1992.
Councilmember Christoph received compensation of approximately
$1,200 in June and $400 in October of 1992.  The contract has been
fully discharged.

The Laguna Beach County Water District has recently announced
its desire to acquire a portion of the city park to construct a
water reservoir and related facilities.  If the city decides not
to voluntarily sell the park land to the water district, the water
district has indicated its intention to initiate an eminent domain
action to condemn the land sought.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in making, or otherwise using their official
position to influence a governmental decision in which they have
an economic interest.  As a city councilmember, Councilmember
Christoph is a public official.  (Section 82048.)

A public official has a financial interest in a decision if
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on the official's economic interests
which is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
(Section 87103.)

Our File No. I-93-294
Page 3


Section 87103 defines a "financial interest" as follows:

An official has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family
or on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

* * * *

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

Section 87103(a), (c) and (d).

You specifically ask whether Councilmember Christoph may
participate in city decisions concerning the disposition of a
portion of a city park and other decisions concerning a proposed
reservoir project. For example, the councilmember is concerned
about city determinations related to general plan consistency,
zoning, and coastal development permit applications.

You also ask whether compensation received by the
councilmember from the city would constitute a potentially
disqualifying economic interest. According to your letter dated
July 28, 1993, Councilmember Christoph has received more than $250
from the city during the past 12 months as an independent
contractor. In our telephone conversation of August 23, 1993, you
also indicated that Councilmember Christoph was not a designated
employee, or otherwise a public official, as a result of the
contractual relationship with the city.

Our File No. I-93-294
Page 4


Compensation from the City

As a general rule, any person or business that has made any
payment to an official in the past 12 months is a source of income
to the official for the purposes of Section 87103(c). Therefore,
Councilmember Christoph must disqualify herself from participating
in any decision which will have a foreseeable and material
financial effect on her sources of income, or any other of her
economic interests.

Salary and reimbursement for expenses or per diem received
from a state, local, or federal government agency is expressly
exempted from the definition of "income" for purposes of the Act.
(Section 82030(b)(2); Battersby Advice Letter, No. A-90-300.)
Consequently, payments from a governmental agency generally do not
create a conflict of interest with respect to decisions affecting
the agency. (Section 87103(c).) However, we have advised that
compensation from a government agency constitutes salary which is
exempted from the definition of "income" under the Act only where
the recipient is an employee, or is otherwise a public official,
of the agency. (Bohigian Advice Letter, No. I-92-280.)

Since Councilmember Christoph was an independent contractor,
not an employee of the city, or otherwise a public official, of
the agency as a result of the contract in question, the
compensation she received from the city is income to her until
October of 1993, which is 12 months after the last payment from
the city of $250 or more.

Regulation 18704 provides a limited exception for purposes of
Section 87103(c) which is applicable to former employers. If
Regulation 18704 applied to Councilmember Christoph's situation,
the City of Laguna Beach would not be considered to a source of
income to her which might lead to a conflict of interest. You
have provided no facts to indicate that Regulation 18704 would
apply to Councilmember Christoph. Therefore, Councilmember
Christoph may not participate in decisions which will have a
reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on the city.


3    Pursuant to Regulation 18704, a former employer is not a source
of income under Section 87103(c) if all of the following apply:
(1) All income from the employer was received by or accrued to
the public official prior to the time he or she became a public
official; (2) The income was received in the normal course of the
previous employment; and (3) There was no expectation by the
public official at the time he or she assumed office of renewed
employment with the former employer. (McCarthy Advice Letter,
No. A-91-100.)

Our File No. I-93-294
Page 5


On the basis of your facts, it appears any decision made will
have a reasonably foreseeable⁴ and material financial effect on
the city. However, the councilmember's disqualification is
required only if the effect is distinguishable from the effect on
the public generally. (Section 87100.) If a decision does not
affect all members of the public in the same manner,
disqualification may be required unless the effect of the decision
on the public official's economic interests is the same as the
effect on a significant segment of the public. (Regulation
18703.) For purposes of our discussion, the public is the entire
population of the City of Laguna Beach. (In re Legan (1985) 9
FPPC Ops. 1; In re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77; Jorgensen Advice
Letter, No. A-90-017.)

We have previously advised that if the effect of a decision
flows to all residents in the jurisdiction in question, the effect
of the decision would be the same as the effect on the "public
generally." (Douglas Advice Letter, No. I-90-128A; Howard Advice
Letter, No. A-92-105; Bohigian Advice Letter, supra.) Therefore,
if the effect of the decisions relating to the disposition of the
city park and the water district's reservoir project flow to all,
or a significant segment,⁵ of the residents of the City of Laguna
Beach, Councilmember Christoph will not be required to disqualify
herself from participating in city council decisions concerning
the city park and other related governmental decisions.




4    The effects of a decision are reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that they will occur. To be
foreseeable, the effects of a decision must be more than a mere
possibility; however certainty is not required. (Downey Care v.
Downey Community Development Com. (1987) 196 Cal. App. 3d 983,
989-991; Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal. App. 3d 817, 822; In re
Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) The Act seeks to prevent more
than just actual conflicts of interest; it also seeks to prevent
even the appearance of a possible conflict of interest. (Witt v.
Morrow, supra at 823.)

5    The Commission has never adopted a strict arithmetic test for
determining what constitutes a significant segment of the public.
However, in order for the "public generally" exception to apply,
the population affected must be large in number and heterogeneous
in nature. (In re Pierro (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 62; Flynn Advice
Letter, No. I-88-430.) We have advised in the past that 18-19
percent of the population of a city constituted a significant
segment of the public. (Morton Advice Letter, No. A-91-374;
Hechtman Advice Letter, No. A-92-469.) Also, please note that the
Commission may be amending Regulation 18703 in the near future.

Our File No. I-93-294
Page 6


Other Interests

It also appears that Councilmember Christoph has an economic
interest in her business, and possibly a joint venture. (Section
82005.) However, also according to your letter dated
July 28, 1993, no decision regarding the disposition of the city
park will have any financial effect whatsoever on Councilmember
Christoph personally or her business interest, the architectural
firm. Therefore, if other city decisions relating to the water
district reservoir project will not have any financial effect on
the councilmember or any of her business interests, it appears
that the city is the councilmember's sole⁶ disqualifying economic
interest.

     We trust this letter provides you with adequate guidance. If
you have any further questions regarding this matter, please feel
free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.⁷

                                   Sincerely,

                                   Wayne Ordos
                                   Executive Director

                                   [signature]
                                   By: Luisa Menchaca
                                       Counsel, Legal Division



6  You have also indicated that although the contract was awarded
jointly to Councilmember Christoph and two other architect/
engineers, each worked on separate components pursuant to the
contract and each was paid separately by the city.
Therefore, for purposes of this analysis, we assume the contract
was executed directly with Councilmember Christoph as an
individual, and therefore, the city is the sole disqualifying
source of income to the official.

7  Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
